content.assessment: Restructuring the categorical architecture and the gatekeeping committee system engages legal authority most directly of all six proposals: SNI/BTMN status is currently defined by EMMI decree 15/2013 with jurisdiction split between county and district-level committees, and any move to a non-categorical tiered model would require statutory or decree-level amendment plus renegotiation of the resource entitlements currently tied to formal classification. This raises significant accountability and enforceability questions, since existing appeal rights (time-limited, administrative-only) and override provisions are calibrated to the current categorical structure and would need reworking to function under a tiered model.
opportunities: Could be designed to simultaneously repair the appeal-timeline and administrative-only recourse limitations currently constraining parental rights
strengths: Targets the structural root of gatekeeping bottlenecks by addressing the single-committee jurisdiction problem embedded in current law
Comparative evidence cited (Wales, Finland) demonstrates that governance restructuring of this kind is legally and administratively feasible elsewhere
threats: Poland's precedent shows that rapid, non-consensual structural reform of this kind can generate severe stakeholder backlash and implementation disruption, a governance risk directly relevant given Hungary's existing committee-based legal architecture
weaknesses: Requires substantial legal-authority change (decree/statute amendment, redefinition of committee jurisdiction) with attendant transition risk and unclear treatment of existing appeal and override rights
No Hungary-specific evidence yet demonstrates that tiered restructuring would resolve the county-level disparities already present in the current system
